CREATE TABLE employees (id INT, name CLOB, department CLOB);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'HR');
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'HR');
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (3, 'Charlie', 'Engineering');
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (4, 'David', 'Engineering');
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (5, 'Eve', 'Marketing');
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees WHERE department = 'HR';
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees WHERE department IN ('HR', 'Marketing');
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees GROUP BY department;
SELECT department, sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY department;
CREATE TABLE departments (id INT, dept_name CLOB);
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (1, 'HR');
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (2, 'Engineering');
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (3, 'Marketing');
SELECT d.dept_name, sys.group_concat(e.name) FROM departments d LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.dept_name = e.department GROUP BY d.dept_name;
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) AS all_employees FROM (SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id > 1) AS subquery;
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM (SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' UNION SELECT name FROM employees WHERE department = 'Marketing') AS combined;
CREATE TABLE projects (proj_id INT, proj_name CLOB, employee_id INT);
INSERT INTO projects VALUES (1, 'Project A', 1);
INSERT INTO projects VALUES (2, 'Project B', 3);
INSERT INTO projects VALUES (3, 'Project C', 4);
SELECT e.department, sys.group_concat(p.proj_name) FROM employees e JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id GROUP BY e.department;
INSERT INTO employees VALUES (6, 'Frank', 'HR');
SELECT sys.group_concat(e.name) FROM employees e LEFT JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id WHERE p.proj_id IS NULL;
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees WHERE LENGTH(name) > 3;
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees WHERE name LIKE 'A%';
SELECT sys.group_concat(name) FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND id < 5;
